ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class; no specific entry for spicy chicken broth ramen with egg. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for spicy chicken ramen with egg. A00LC (noodle soup dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific ramen dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of spicy chicken broth ramen with egg, approximately 550 g, as typically served at a Japanese ramen restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup with meat and egg — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; adapted for spicy chicken broth variant
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for ramen noodle soup (NDB 22899) and chicken broth (NDB 06172), combined with egg (NDB 01123) and typical spicy ramen toppings. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of broth (~55%), noodles (~25%), protein toppings (~12%), egg (~8%). Values are consistent with published ramen nutritional analyses in peer-reviewed literature (e.g., Tsuji et al., Journal of Nutritional Science and Vitaminology). Sodium is elevated due to seasoned broth and spicy tare.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and ramen-type noodle soups — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy chicken broth ramen with egg. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based Asian noodles (GI range 40–61, mean ~52–58) and ramen noodle soup analogues. The protein and fat content of the broth and egg lower the effective GI relative to plain noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 x 46.75) / 100 = 25.7, where carbohydrate per 550g serving = 8.5 x 5.5 = 46.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~2,640 mg per bowl from seasoned broth and spicy tare)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100g (approximately 2,640 mg per standard 550g bowl), which substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the broth can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles; protein and fat from egg and broth partially attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl provides approximately 46.75g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 25.7, which is in the high GL range; portion control, choosing a smaller serving, and pairing with additional protein or vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth, egg yolk, and meat toppings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish with caution due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (~4.4g per bowl), which may contribute to blood pressure elevation and unfavourable lipid profiles; limiting broth consumption and choosing leaner protein toppings are recommended strategies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Chicken Broth Ramen with Egg (辛口鶏白湯ラーメン卵入り) is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of springy wheat-based ramen noodles served in a rich, spicy chicken paitan (white chicken bone broth) seasoned with a spicy tare (seasoning sauce) made from chili paste, soy sauce, and aromatics. The dish is topped with a soft-boiled marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), which contributes a creamy, custard-like yolk, along with typical ramen accompaniments such as chashu (braised pork or chicken), nori (dried seaweed), bamboo shoots (menma), and sliced green onions. The broth is characteristically opaque, rich, and umami-forward with a pronounced spicy heat. As served at Ramen Matsuri (7 North Canal Road, Singapore), this dish is priced at SGD 19.95 and represents a restaurant-quality Japanese ramen experience. Nutritionally, a standard 550g bowl provides approximately 429 kcal, 28.6g protein, 15.4g fat, and 46.75g carbohydrate, with a very high sodium content of approximately 2,640 mg. The glycemic load per bowl is approximately 25.7, placing it in the high glycemic load category. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension due to its very high sodium content, and requires carbohydrate awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The egg contributes cholesterol and high-quality protein, while the chicken broth provides collagen-derived amino acids. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or egg allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for this specific dish (Spicy Chicken Broth Ramen with Egg from Ramen Matsuri) exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ramen and chicken broth entries combined with egg data, consistent with peer-reviewed ramen nutritional analyses. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using analogous wheat noodle soup entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. The input food_group was listed as 'Dairy and eggs', which is incorrect for a ramen dish; corrected to 'Mixed' as this is a composite noodle soup dish. 2. The food_name 'Spicy with egg' from the input is a menu shorthand; canonical name expanded to 'Spicy Chicken Broth Ramen with Egg' based on the description field. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy ramen composite source. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy ramen composite source. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy ramen composite source. 6. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in chicken broth and egg-based ramen per USDA data. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists. 8. Sodium value of 480 mg per 100g reflects typical seasoned ramen broth sodium levels as documented in peer-reviewed literature and USDA broth data; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 9. The dish contains gluten (wheat noodles), egg, soy (tare), and possibly sesame; not suitable for those with these allergies. 10. Halal status is unconfirmed for Ramen Matsuri; is_halal set to false by default.
